title: Chiral anomaly and planar Hall conductance in pseudospin-$1$ Fermions
abstract: Positive longitudinal magnetoconductance (LMC) and planar Hall conductance (PHC) are hallmark transport signatures of the chiral anomaly in Weyl semimetals. Recent theoretical developments have extended Weyl fermions to multifold Fermionic systems with higher-pseudospin quasiparticle excitations, motivating the study of their magnetotransport properties. Here, we employ semiclassical Boltzmann transport theory within the relaxation-time approximation to investigate magnetotransport in pseudospin-1 Weyl semimetals, incorporating momentum-dependent scattering, orbital magnetic moment corrections, and charge-conservation constraints. To obtain a finite PHC, we break azimuthal symmetry through either a generic tilt of the quasiparticle dispersion or a finite misalignment between the electric and magnetic fields. In the untilted case, the PHC is positive and scales quadratically with magnetic field strength. Increasing the scattering strength induces a sign reversal of the PHC, producing a transition from positive to negative values. The PHC further exhibits the characteristic angular dependence $\sin 2γ$, where $γ$ is the angle between the magnetic field and the $x$-axis. Tilt qualitatively alters this behavior: tilt along the $x$- and $z$-directions changes the angular response to $\sinγ$ and $\cosγ$, respectively, generating strong anisotropy in the planar Hall signal. Moreover, the PHC shows a nonmonotonic dependence on tilt magnitude, revealing the interplay between tilt-induced symmetry breaking and chiral-anomaly-driven transport. Our results provide experimentally accessible signatures of multifold fermions and a framework for interpreting magnetotransport measurements in candidate materials of space groups 199, 214, and 220.
summary: This theoretical study analyzes how chiral anomalies manifest as measurable transport signals (PHC and LMC) in advanced systems like pseudospin-1 Weyl semimetals. By incorporating complex effects such as momentum-dependent scattering and dispersion tilting, the authors predict characteristic angular anisotropies and sign reversals in these magnetotransport coefficients. The findings offer concrete, experimentally testable signatures for identifying multifold fermion materials.
main_problem: The study investigates magnetotransport signatures, specifically Planar Hall Conductance (PHC) and Longitudinal Magnetoconductance (LMC), arising from the chiral anomaly in systems with higher-pseudospin quasiparticle excitations.
main_result: The PHC exhibits characteristic angular dependencies ($\sin 2\gamma$ without tilt, $\sin \gamma$ or $\cos \gamma$ with tilt) and shows a sign reversal when scattering strength increases. Tilt introduces strong anisotropy to the planar Hall signal.
method: Semiclassical Boltzmann transport theory is employed within the relaxation-time approximation, incorporating momentum-dependent scattering and orbital magnetic moment corrections.
model_or_system: The model uses pseudospin-1 Weyl semimetals characterized by a Lagrangian density describing multifold chiral quasiparticles. The analysis accounts for both generic tilt of the dispersion and misalignment between E and B fields to break azimuthal symmetry.
key_observables: Planar Hall Conductance (PHC), Longitudinal Magnetoconductance (LMC), angular dependence ($\sin 2\gamma$, $\sin \gamma$, $\cos \gamma$), and nonmonotonic dependence on tilt magnitude.
important_parameters: Scattering strength ($\alpha$), magnetic field angle ($\gamma$), and the tilt parameters ($t_x, t_z$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on semiclassical dynamics and assumes that the Lorentz-force contribution can be neglected when $\omega_c 	au \ll 1$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate LMC and PHC dependence on scattering strength ($\alpha$) and field angle ($\gamma$), demonstrating sign reversals. Further figures map out the anisotropy of PHC as a function of tilt components ($t_x, t_z$) and angle ($\gamma$).
paper_structure: The paper builds from general theoretical frameworks (Hamiltonian/Lagrangian) to specific transport calculations using the Boltzmann equation. It systematically analyzes the effects of symmetry breaking mechanisms—namely, scattering strength variation and explicit dispersion tilting—on the resulting magnetoconductivity components.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed, quantitative framework for interpreting complex magnetotransport measurements in topological materials by explicitly modeling higher-order pseudospin degrees of freedom and their coupling to external fields.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. We investigate the LMC and PHC in a pseudospin-1 semimetal, both in the presence and absence of the OMM, for two representative values of the field orientation angle: γ = π/2 and γ = π/4. Our analysis reveals that an increase in the relative strength of internode (intervalley) scattering, quantified by the parameter α, leads to a sign reversal in both LMC and PHC, from positive to negative. Furthermore, in configurations where the electric and magnetic fields are non-collinear, the critical scattering strength αc required to induce this reversal decreases progressively as γ deviates from the orthogonal limit (γ = π/2). This behavior underscores the sensitivity of magnetotransport...

Figure 2. Phase plot of LMC and PHC for pseudospin-1 fermions with respect to intervalley scattering strength α and magnetic field angle γ measured from increasing x-direction. While LMC has its maxima at π/2, PHC vanishes for this parallel field configuration. White dotted curves separate the region having opposing signs of conductivities, confirm- ing the dependence αc(γ): αc ∼sin2 γ in LMC. PHC van- ishes for both the field orientations: γ = 0 and γ = π/2 as plotted in (b), shows sign reversal above a fixed value of αP HC c > αLMC c . LMC is normalized by zero field value, i.e., σ ′ zz = (σzz(B = 0.50 T) −σzz(B = 0 T))/σzz(B = 0 T), while PHC is not normalized.

Figure 3. (a-b) The LMC and PHC are analyzed as functions of the tilt along the x-axis and the orientation angle γ of the applied magnetic field relative to the ˆx-axis, for a constant magnetic field strength of B = 0.5 T and α = 0.20. In this analysis, the cone tilt directions are assumed to be oppositely oriented. (c) PHC is plotted as a function of tilt tx. PHC shows a non-monotonic behavior and is an odd function of tilt, attaining its extrema at |tx| = 0.50. Along the direction of the arrow, the γ is varied from 0 to π/2. PHC is normalized to its maximum value and LMC as in Fig. 2(a).

Figure 4. (a-b) The LMC and PHC are analyzed as functions of the tilt along the z-axis and the orientation angle γ of the applied magnetic field relative to the ˆz-axis, for a constant magnetic field strength of B = 0.5 T. In this analysis, the tilt directions of the cones are considered to be oppositely oriented. (c) PHC as a function of tilt tz at different values of γ.

Figure 5. (a) The angular dependence of the PHC is inves- tigated for varying values of the tilt parameter tz. The in- set corresponds to the untilted case (tz = 0), exhibiting the conventional sin 2γ dependence characteristic of systems with preserved azimuthal symmetry. Introducing a finite tilt modi- fies this angular behavior, progressively transitioning towards a cos γ dependence as tz increases in magnitude. Specifically, along the indicated direction in the plot, the ratio tz/vF is systematically varied from −0.5 to 0, where the tilt orienta- tion is considered to be oppositely aligned for the two cones, emphasizing the role of tilt anisotropy in shaping the PHC response.
